title: The understudy
summary: An understudy who has waited three years for a chance to go on, and the lead actress, talk on the night before the lead's final performance.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: You wanted to see me?

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: Yes. Sit down, please. I want to talk to you before tomorrow night.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: About the show?

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: About what happens after the show. Tomorrow is my last performance. The role is yours after that. The producers told me yesterday.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: Officially?

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: Officially. From Wednesday, you open the new run. You have been ready for two years.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: Three.

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: Three. I am sorry I did not get sick.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: Don't joke about that.

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: I'm not joking. Most understudies wait for their lead to fail. You waited and you didn't, even when you could have. I noticed.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: What was I going to do? You were good.

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: That is not the only choice an understudy has. You watched me run the same lines two hundred and forty times. You corrected nothing. You suggested nothing. You let me make my own mistakes for three years.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: It was your role.

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: Yes. And tomorrow it will be yours. Which is why I am here. I want to tell you what I would have wanted someone to tell me when I started.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: Please.

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: Don't try to be me. The audience will compare you to me for about a week. Then they will forget. Spend that week being exactly what you are, not what they remember. If you spend the week imitating me, you will spend the next two years being a worse version of me. The play will die that way.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: I have been preparing for three years.

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: I know. That is the other thing I want to say. The preparation has been good. It has also been very long. The first week, you will be frightened. That is normal. The second week, the part will start to be yours, and you will think you have been pretending all along. That is also normal. Don't let it move you.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: How do you know all this?

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: I took over from someone else, eight years ago, when I was where you are now. She told me almost exactly these things in this dressing room. I thought she was being kind. She was being precise.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: Why are you stopping? Truly.

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: Two reasons. My body is not the same. The third act takes me ninety minutes to recover from now. And I have started reading the lines instead of meaning them. The audience cannot always tell. I can.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: I will remember that.

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: Good. One last thing. The night you start to read the lines instead of meaning them — find your understudy and have this conversation.

body.speaker: Mira
body.text: Promise.

body.speaker: Anh
body.text: Now go home. Tomorrow you watch me one more time. Then you are not an understudy anymore.

annotations.meaningVi: diễn viên dự bị (đóng thay vai chính khi cần)
hồi ba (phần ba của vở kịch)

grammarNotes.title: Conditional and modal hedging
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Anh dùng nhiều dạng giả định để khuyên Mira mà không áp đặt. 'If you spend the week imitating me, you will spend the next two years being a worse version of me.' (Câu điều kiện loại 1 — hậu quả thực tế.) 'Don't let it move you.' (Mệnh lệnh nhẹ.) 'You will be frightened.' (Tương lai dự đoán, không phải mệnh lệnh.) Lưu ý cách câu vừa khuyên vừa giữ tự do cho Mira.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Anh blends conditionals and gentle imperatives for advice. First conditional for real consequences: 'If you spend the week imitating me, you will spend the next two years being a worse version of me.' Future for prediction: 'You will be frightened.' Mild imperative: 'Don't let it move you.' She advises without commanding.

translationVi:
Mira: Chị gọi em ạ?
Anh: Ừ. Ngồi đi. Chị muốn nói với em trước tối mai.
Mira: Về vở diễn ạ?
Anh: Về chuyện sau vở diễn. Mai là buổi cuối của chị. Sau đó, vai sẽ là của em. Các nhà sản xuất báo hôm qua.
Mira: Chính thức rồi ạ?
Anh: Chính thức. Từ thứ Tư, em mở đợt diễn mới. Em đã sẵn sàng hai năm rồi.
Mira: Ba năm.
Anh: Ba năm. Chị xin lỗi vì đã không ốm.
Mira: Đừng đùa kiểu đó.
Anh: Chị không đùa. Đa số diễn viên dự bị chờ vai chính sẩy chân. Em chờ và em không làm vậy, dù em hoàn toàn có thể. Chị để ý mà.
Mira: Em làm vậy để làm gì? Chị diễn tốt.
Anh: Đó không phải lựa chọn duy nhất của một dự bị. Em đã xem chị chạy đi chạy lại đúng những câu thoại đó hai trăm bốn mươi lần. Em không sửa gì. Em không gợi ý gì. Em để chị mắc sai lầm của chính chị suốt ba năm.
Mira: Đó là vai của chị mà.
Anh: Đúng. Và mai nó sẽ là của em. Chính vì thế chị ở đây. Chị muốn nói với em điều mà chị từng ước có ai đó nói với chị khi chị mới bắt đầu.
Mira: Chị nói đi ạ.
Anh: Đừng cố làm chị. Khán giả sẽ so sánh em với chị khoảng một tuần. Rồi họ sẽ quên. Hãy dành tuần đó để là đúng cái em đang là, chứ không phải cái họ nhớ. Nếu em dành tuần đó để bắt chước chị, em sẽ dành hai năm tiếp theo để làm một phiên bản tồi hơn chị. Vở diễn sẽ chết theo cách đó.
Mira: Em đã chuẩn bị ba năm.
Anh: Chị biết. Đó cũng là điều thứ hai chị muốn nói. Sự chuẩn bị đã tốt. Nó cũng đã rất dài. Tuần đầu, em sẽ sợ. Bình thường thôi. Tuần thứ hai, vai diễn bắt đầu thật sự là của em, và em sẽ thấy mình đã giả vờ từ đầu. Cái đó cũng bình thường. Đừng để nó lay chuyển em.
Mira: Sao chị biết hết những điều này?
Anh: Chị nhận vai từ một người khác, tám năm trước, khi chị ở đúng chỗ em đang đứng. Cô ấy nói với chị gần như chính xác những điều này trong cái phòng thay đồ này. Chị tưởng cô ấy đang tử tế. Cô ấy đang cụ thể.
Mira: Sao chị dừng? Thật lòng đi.
Anh: Hai lý do. Cơ thể chị không còn như trước. Sau hồi ba, chị mất chín mươi phút để hồi sức. Và chị đã bắt đầu đọc thoại thay vì có ý nói chúng. Khán giả không phải lúc nào cũng nhận ra. Chị thì có.
Mira: Em sẽ nhớ điều đó.
Anh: Tốt. Một điều cuối. Cái đêm em bắt đầu đọc thoại thay vì có ý nói chúng — tìm dự bị của em và có cuộc trò chuyện này.
Mira: Em hứa.
Anh: Giờ về nhà đi. Mai em xem chị thêm một lần. Sau đó em không còn là dự bị nữa.

questions.prompt: Why has Anh asked to talk to Mira?
options: To tell her she will officially take over the role from Wednesday and to give her advice.
To replace her with another understudy.
To ask her opinion of the script.
questions.explanation: 'Tomorrow is my last performance. The role is yours after that.'

questions.prompt: How long has Mira been an understudy?
Anh says, 'I am sorry I did not get sick.' What does she mean?
options: She is half-joking that an understudy usually gets a chance only when the lead is unable to perform.
She is apologising for poor health.
She regrets not taking sick days.
She is annoyed at the producers.
questions.explanation: She follows the line with 'Most understudies wait for their lead to fail.'

questions.prompt: What did Mira NOT do during the three years, that Anh notices?
options: She did not correct or suggest anything; she let Anh make her own mistakes.
She did not learn the lines.
She did not speak to the producers.
questions.explanation: 'You corrected nothing. You suggested nothing. You let me make my own mistakes for three years.'
questions.hint: Anh's longer reply about what Mira did and didn't do.

questions.prompt: What is Anh's main piece of advice about how Mira should play the role?
options: Don't try to be Anh — be exactly what you are, not what the audience remembers.
questions.explanation: 'Don't try to be me. … Spend that week being exactly what you are, not what they remember.'

questions.prompt: What does Anh predict will happen in Mira's second week?
options: The part will start to be hers, and she will feel she has been pretending all along.
questions.explanation: 'The second week, the part will start to be yours, and you will think you have been pretending all along.'
questions.hint: Anh predicts the second week in detail.

questions.prompt: How does Anh know exactly what Mira will feel?
options: She took over from someone else eight years ago and was told nearly the same things.
She read it in a book.
She has been told by a director.
questions.explanation: 'I took over from someone else, eight years ago, when I was where you are now. She told me almost exactly these things in this dressing room.'
questions.hint: Look at Anh's reply about how she knows.

questions.prompt: Why is Anh leaving the role?
options: Her body needs ninety minutes to recover from the third act, and she has started 'reading the lines instead of meaning them.'
questions.explanation: Anh gives 'two reasons,' both quoted in her long answer near the end.

questions.prompt: What does Anh ask Mira to do when Mira herself starts to 'read the lines instead of meaning them'?
options: Find her own understudy and have the same conversation.
questions.explanation: 'Find your understudy and have this conversation.'

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes the kind of conversation this is?
options: A handover — one professional passing on hard-won, specific knowledge to her successor.
A farewell that avoids any practical advice.
A test to see if Mira knows the lines.
questions.explanation: Anh inherits and passes on practical, exact advice — a chain of handovers.
questions.hint: Notice the pattern: Anh was told the same things eight years ago.

cloze.template:
Mira: You wanted to see me?
Anh: Yes. Sit down, please. I want to talk to you before tomorrow night.
Mira: About the show?
Anh: About what happens after the show. Tomorrow is my last {{b1}}. The role is yours after that. The producers told me yesterday.
Mira: Officially?
Anh: Officially. From Wednesday, you open the new run. You have been {{b2}} for two years.
Mira: Three.
Anh: Three. I am sorry I did not get {{b3}}.
Mira: Don't joke about that.
Anh: I'm not joking. Most understudies wait for their lead to {{b4}}. You waited and you didn't, even when you could have. I noticed.
Mira: What was I going to do? You were good.
Anh: That is not the only choice an understudy has. You watched me run the same lines two hundred and forty times. You corrected nothing. You suggested nothing. You let me make my own {{b5}} for three years.
Mira: It was your role.
Anh: Yes. And tomorrow it will be yours. Which is why I am here. I want to tell you what I would have wanted someone to tell me when I started.
Mira: Please.
Anh: Don't try to be me. The audience will compare you to me for about a {{b6}}. Then they will forget. Spend that week being exactly what you are, not what they remember. If you spend the week {{b7}} me, you will spend the next two years being a worse version of me. The play will die that way.
Mira: I have been preparing for three years.
Anh: I know. That is the other thing I want to say. The preparation has been good. It has also been very long. The first week, you will be {{b8}}. That is normal. The second week, the part will start to be yours, and you will think you have been pretending all along. That is also normal. Don't let it move you.
Mira: How do you know all this?
Anh: I took over from someone else, eight years ago, when I was where you are now. She told me almost exactly these things in this dressing room. I thought she was being kind. She was being {{b9}}.
Mira: Why are you stopping? Truly.
Anh: Two reasons. My body is not the same. The third act takes me ninety minutes to {{b10}} from now. And I have started reading the lines instead of {{b11}} them. The audience cannot always tell. I can.
Mira: I will remember that.
Anh: Good. One last thing. The night you start to read the lines instead of meaning them — find your {{b12}} and have this conversation.
Mira: Promise.
Anh: Now go home. Tomorrow you watch me one more time. Then you are not an understudy anymore.

blanks.explanation: Mira has been ready (for two — actually three — years).
Anh's wry apology: she did not get sick.
Most understudies wait for the lead to fail.
Mira let Anh make her own mistakes.
The audience will compare them for about a week.
Don't spend the week imitating me.
The first week, you will be frightened.
Anh now realises her predecessor was being precise, not kind.
The third act takes her ninety minutes to recover from.
Reading the lines instead of meaning them.
Mira should find her own understudy when the time comes.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Anh tells Mira that the previous lead was 'being precise' rather than 'being kind.' Has someone in your life given you advice that felt cold at the time but turned out to be precise? Why is precision easier to recognise in hindsight than in the moment?
